english: middle part of a widow's drawers prevents epilepsy and heals wounds. — 2. W. = dór-ma? (dor ma?)

1. net, esp. fishing-net, *tám-pa* (tam pa) to spread, to fix it C., W.; (nya-)dól-pa ((nya-)dol pa) a fisherman, cf. rdól-pa (rdol pa). — 2. W. stew-pan. — 3. dol yčód-pa (dol gcod pa) to split, to cleave Sch.

a load (of a beast of burden) that has to be carried by compulsory service, without being paid for; kál-dós (khal dos) id.; j́a-dós (ja dos) a load of tea carried in this manner; dos gél-ba (dos 'gel ba) to load (on), to pack, dos bógs-pa (dos 'bogs pa) (not pbóg-pa (pbog pa) Cs.) to unload; dós-pa (dos pa) a conductor of such loads Cs., dos-dpon (dos dpon) the leader of a caravan of such loads; dos drág-pa (dos drag pa) 1. Mil. prob.: hard compulsory service; 2. perh. also: severe in exacting it, e.g. a feudal lord.
a small copper coin, used in the western part of the Himalaya, a thick paisa, of the value of half a penny.

I. sbst. 1. grate, lattice; net, net-work, lús-la drá-bar brel (lus la dra bar 'brel) (the veins) are spread throughout the body like net-work S.g.; rús-pai drá-ba (rus pa'i dra ba) the frame-work of bones, the skeleton Thgy.; ͵od-zér-gyi drá-la ('od zer gyi dra la) a pencil or aggregate of rays of light (lit. lattice-work of rays) Glr.; dra mig (dra mig) id., esp. col.; lčágs-(kyi) dra(-mig) (lcags (kyi) dra (mig)) iron railings; grate; gridiron; rgyá-dra (rgya dra) wooden rails, fence C., W.; dra-(ba) pýed(-pa) (dra (ba) phyed (pa)) Lex., Glr. 'half-lattice', technical term for a kind of silk ornament; drá-ba-čan (dra ba can) latticed, grated; dra-lag-drá-lag-čan (dra lag dra lag can) having many forked ends or branches, of the horns of a stag. — 2. a bag made of net-work Cs., dra-pád (dra phad), dra-čúṅ (dra chung) id. — 3. the web of water-fowls. II. vb., pf. dras (dras), W. *dé-če* (*de che*), to cut, clip, lop, dress, prune, pare (leather, cloth, paper, wings etc. with knife or scissors); also fig.: pai miṅ-nas drás-te (pa'i ming nas dras te) borrowing (a syllable) from the father's name Glr. (twice); cf. also Tar. 107, 13; *téb-dhe-pa* (*teb dhe pa*) C. one that cuts the strings (of a purse) on his thumb, i.e. a cut-purse, pick-pocket; gos-drás (gos dras) cloth cut out for a garment Cs.; dras-spyád (dras spyad) scissors Sch.; dra-gri (dra gri) Cs.: 'a tailor's knife used for shears'; drai (sic) ro (dra'i (sic) ro) Sch., *dé-rūg* (*de rug*), ta-dé* (*ta de*) W. clippings, cuttings, remnants.
experienced, practised, learned Sch.; so perh. Pth., where however bra-ma (bra ma) and tra-ma (khra ma) is the usual form.
or *dra-su* (*dra su*) W. a small pan with a handle; a ladle.

1. W. the post; any parcels or goods conveyed by post, the Hind. डाक. — 2. expedient, profitable, of use, púl-ba drág-gam (phul ba drag gam) will it be of any use, well-applied, if I give? Mil.; j̀i byas kyaṅ ma drag (ji byas kyang ma drag) whatever I did, it was of no use Pth.; na c̀i-ltar byás-na drag (na ci ltar byas na drag) what course will it be expedient to take? what shall I do best? Pth.; *c̀i dhag, ghaṅ dhag* (*ci dhag, ghang dhag*) C. what is right? what is expedient? nád-pa drág-pas čog (nad pa drag pas chog) it is sufficient, if the patient is getting better Mil.

1. noble, of noble birth C., *drág-po* (*drag po*) W.; mi drág-pa (mi drag pa), or merely drág-pa (drag pa), a nobleman; drag-rígs (drag rigs) nobility, gentry; drág-par byéd-pa (drag par byed pa) to raise to nobility, drág-par gyúr-ba (drag par gyur ba) to become a nobleman Cs.; drag-dós (drag dos) an inferior officer or magistrate Cs. — 2. gen. drágs-po (drags po), W. also drag-čàn (drag can), (Ssk. तीव्र, उग्र) strong, vehement, violent ču drag-pa (chu drag pa) a rapid river, violent current; brtson-grus drag-pa ͵bád-pa (brtson 'grus drag pa 'bad pa) or ͵dún-pa drag-pa ('dun pa drag pa) unbending, unwearied application; skad drag-pa (skad drag pa) a powerful voice; Krims drag-pa (khrims drag pa) a severe punishment; snyiṅ-rj́e drag (snying rje drag) yearning compassion; strong, forcible, of expressions or language; moreover an epithet of terrifying deities, particularly of Siwa (Ssk. रुद्र), drag-mo (drag mo) fem.; z̀i rgyas dbaṅ drag (zhi rgyas dbang drag) v. sub z̀i-ba (zhi ba). — Adv. drag-tu (drag tu) vehemently, violently, e.g. to pull, to lament, to implore; hastily, speedily, e.g. to come Wdn.; drág-por (drag por), e.g. drág-por bcàd-de bklág-par byao (drag por bcad de bklag par bya'o) in reading a marked stop should be made Gram.; ha-čaṅ mi-drág-par (ha cang mi drag par) very gently, softly; drág-gis (drag gis), dád-pa (dad pa) to believe firmly Mil. — 3. drág-pa pos-
